---
title: "Single Cell Analysis of Alcohol Liver Disease"
subtitle: "BNFO 420 Capstone - Group 4"
---

## One-paragraph summary
This project examines whether immune cell populations show distinct gene-expression changes in alcohol-related liver disease (ALD) compared with healthy liver and whether these changes may contribute to disease pathology. To answer this question, we analyzed human single-cell RNA sequencing data from 5 ALD liver samples and 5 healthy control liver samples. Using a Seurat-based workflow, we performed quality control, normalization, dimensionality reduction, clustering, marker-gene annotation, differential expression analysis, cell-type proportion analysis, and gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA). This approach allowed us to evaluate both changes in cell abundance and changes in cell-specific transcriptional activity across disease conditions.
Our results show that the immune response in ALD is not uniform. Instead, different immune cell populations display distinct and biologically meaningful patterns of dysfunction. T cells showed the strongest transcriptional changes, increased substantially in proportion, and lost key cytotoxic effector programs. NK cells also showed major suppression of cytotoxic pathways and decreased in abundance, suggesting weakening of the innate cytotoxic immune response. B cells showed reduced humoral immune and complement-related activity. Monocytes and Kupffer cells showed evidence of inflammatory metabolic reprogramming. In contrast, conventional dendritic cells upregulated antigen presentation and T-cell priming pathways, suggesting continued immune stimulation despite broader immune dysfunction. Across the dataset, complement suppression was the most consistent shared signal, appearing across both immune and non-immune cell populations and suggesting widespread disruption of immune homeostasis in the liver.
These findings support the idea that immune dysfunction in ALD is not simply a secondary effect of liver injury, but may actively contribute to disease progression. This interpretation is strengthened by hepatocyte findings, including reduced expression of key functional markers such as ALB and APOB. This may suggest impaired normal liver function in ALD. Final figures include UMAP visualizations, PCA plots, cell-type proportion comparisons, marker-gene dot plots, differential expression plots, and pathway enrichment figures, all of which help illustrate how immune-cell dysfunction and hepatocyte impairment interact in ALD. Limitations include the nature of the dataset and the need to account for partial immune-transcript contamination in hepatocyte analyses.



## Project roadmap (living)
- [x] Find dataset(s)  
  Identified and used GEO dataset (GSE236382) with single-cell RNA sequencing data from ALD liver samples, and added a matched healthy control dataset for comparison.

- [x] Define outcome + predictors / variables  
  Outcome: disease status (ALD vs Control)  
  Predictors: gene expression levels across individual cells, cluster identity (cell type), and pathway enrichment scores  

- [x] Exploratory analysis + QC  
  Performed quality control filtering (mitochondrial content, gene counts), normalization, and identification of highly variable genes. Generated QC plots and ensured data quality prior to analysis.

- [x] Method 1 (required)  
  Applied Seurat workflow including PCA, UMAP, clustering, and marker gene analysis to identify liver cell populations and compare expression patterns between ALD and control.

- [x] Method 2 (optional/extension)  
  Performed differential expression analysis followed by gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) to identify significantly altered biological pathways.

- [x] Final figures + interpretation  
  Generated UMAP plots, marker gene dot plots, and pathway enrichment figures. Interpreted results to show increased immune activation and disruption of metabolic pathways in ALD.

- [ ] Limitations + ethics + reproducibility notes  
  To be finalized; will include discussion of dataset size, global vs cell-type-specific analysis, and reproducibility through shared code and data sources.
  
## Where to find things
- **Scripts:** `scripts/`
- **Data notes:** `data/README.md`
- **Figures:** `figures/`
- **Citations:** `references.bib`
